subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: triangulated across multilateral bodies (IMF Article IV, OECD, World Justice Project, RSF, Transparency International) + Irish official stats (CSO, Central Bank, Fiscal Council) + Irish press (Irish Times, RTE, The Journal).

Economic reality is read via GNI* (modified gross national income), not GDP, which multinational/IP accounting massively distorts; corporate-tax receipts are dangerously concentrated in a few US multinationals - a real fiscal vulnerability.
Caveat: Ireland is itself Anglophone, so the 'language of the place' offers no outside-language check; EU/Eurostat/ECB were the available non-Anglophone cross-checks.

economic.notes: Strong headline numbers built on a concentrated, externally exposed multinational base; best read via GNI*.

fiscal_state.notes: Headline surpluses (2025 ~3.3% of GNI*) funded by highly concentrated multinational corporate tax; excluding that windfall the underlying balance is a deficit; two sovereign savings funds are being seeded. Read via GNI*, not GDP. (IMF 2026 Art IV; Irish Fiscal Advisory Council)

monetary_independence.notes: Eurozone - the ECB is credible and independent; no national monetary control. (ECB)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: Eurozone member; the euro is the #2 global reserve currency, a modest shared benefit. (IMF COFER)

inflation.notes: HICP ~3.6% (Apr 2026), near the euro-area top on energy; projected back toward 2% by ~2028. (CSO, Central Bank of Ireland)

banking_stability.notes: Rebuilt post-2008; a concentrated three-pillar system (AIB/BoI/PTSB) with sound liquidity; leveraged finance and commercial real estate the watch-items. (IMF; Central Bank of Ireland)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: A pharma/tech export hub acutely exposed to US tariff and tax-reform risk (2026 Section 232 pharma tariffs; OECD Pillar Two); a handful of US multinationals drive exports and corporate-tax receipts - a real concentration vulnerability. No resource curse. (Irish Times; Fiscal Council)

institutional.notes: An excellent common-law baseline and pioneering statistics, with mild press-ownership and political-finance watch-items.

rule_of_law.notes: WJP #8/143; a strong common-law system; mild civil-justice weakening noted. (WJP 2025)

statistical_integrity.notes: The CSO is independent and high-quality; it pioneered GNI* to correct the GDP distortion. (CSO, Eurostat)

civil_service_capacity.notes: Capable, but with housing/infrastructure delivery and health-service execution gaps; Transparency International flags political-finance 'dark money'. (TI Ireland 2025; OECD)

press_freedom.notes: RSF ~#15 (2025, 'good'), but highly concentrated media ownership, an RTE funding crisis, and a 2025 defamation reform critics call weak on anti-SLAPP. (RSF; Index on Censorship)

political_social.notes: A stable governing core and a standout citizenship-by-descent route, against rising migration-driven social strain.

political_stability.notes: FF-FG coalition continuity after the Nov 2024 election; an orderly Oct 2025 presidential election; peaceful, predictable governance. (RTE, Irish Times)

civil_liberties.notes: Strong common-law protections; robust expression and assembly. (WJP, Amnesty)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: A standout lawful, durable, English-speaking route via citizenship by descent (an Ireland-born grandparent on the Foreign Births Register confers full citizenship); general immigration is harder (Critical Skills permit, Stamp 0). (Department of Foreign Affairs; Citizens Information)

social_cohesion.notes: A 2023 Dublin riot, 2025 anti-asylum-centre unrest, and 2026 protests with far-right involvement; but surveys find the far-right view is not the majority and attitudes trend inclusive. (RTE; Al Jazeera)

demographics.notes: Younger than EU peers (median ~39) and still growing; the acute housing shortage is the structural drag. (Eurostat, CSO)

geopolitical.notes: A committed EU member but militarily neutral with thin defence capacity.

alliance_reliability.notes: A committed EU member (holding the EU Council presidency in H2 2026) but militarily neutral and non-NATO, with thin defence capacity off a very low base. (European Commission; Budget 2026)

conflict_involvement.notes: No direct conflict, but tail exposure via undersea cables (much transatlantic data passes near Irish waters) and Russian activity, with very limited naval patrol capacity. (Atlantic Council)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: EU sanctions regime; free capital mobility, no domestic controls. (EU)

physical_practical.notes: A mild climate and low violent crime, against an acute housing/health/infrastructure strain.

climate.notes: Mild maritime, high readiness, low heat exposure - but the most AMOC-exposed state; a slowdown could sharply cool/destabilize NW Europe (flag the tail). (ND-GAIN; IPCC AMOC)

healthcare.notes: The HSE is universal-ish but two-tier and costly, with record waiting lists breaching reform targets. (HSE; IHCA)

infrastructure.notes: The housing crisis is the dominant negative (completions rising but far below need); the grid is strained by data-centre demand. (CSO, ESRI, CRU)

disaster_insurance.notes: A material flood-cover gap (~1 in 20 buildings with limited or no flood insurance), projected to widen. (Central Bank of Ireland)

summary: Strong common-law institutions, a young (by European standards) and growing population, and a standout English-speaking citizenship-by-descent route, with headline budget surpluses. But the fiscal and export strength rests on a dangerously concentrated multinational corporate-tax base (best read via GNI*, not GDP) exposed to US tariff and tax-reform risk; an acute housing shortage is the dominant domestic drag; and the country is militarily neutral with thin defence capacity.
